hero.headline: Software developer with infrastructure roots.
hero.subline: Early-career, systems-aware, and moving deliberately deeper into software development.
hero.name: Daniël

foundation.heading: A bit about me
paragraphs: Most developers start with code. I started with infrastructure: live systems, support environments, and technical operations where reliability is not optional.
That foundation still shapes how I build software. I think about dependencies, failure modes, and maintainability as part of the build process, not as an afterthought.

capabilities.heading: What I build
capabilities.intro: An honest map of current areas, foundations, and active growth.

groups.title: Software development
groups.note: Building APIs and web applications with growing focus on structure and reliability.

groups.title: Infrastructure and systems
groups.note: This foundation informs systems thinking across everything I build.

groups.title: Tooling and environment
groups.note: Not a DevOps profile, but infrastructure-aware by background.

work.heading: Featured projects
work.intro: The portfolio is still growing, but the direction is already clear: infrastructure-aware engineering, backend security, and software that solves real operational problems.

references.title: OpenCaptive
references.summary: An open-source captive portal platform connecting networking fundamentals with SaaS architecture — authentication flows, multi-tenancy, and access management built from an infrastructure-first perspective.

references.title: Aegis.Auth
references.summary: A reusable .NET authentication and authorization library focused on JWT, RBAC, and claims-based identity — security tooling built for developers rather than end users.

about.headline: Infrastructure first. Software by intent.
about.opening: Before I wrote software, I worked with the systems software depends on: networking, Linux, infrastructure support, and practical troubleshooting in real environments.

sections.title: Where the technical foundation came from
paragraphs: My technical foundation started in support and infrastructure contexts where systems are live and consequences are immediate.
That environment taught me to stay calm, diagnose carefully, and understand how one change affects everything connected to it.

sections.title: What that background gave me
paragraphs: It gave me systems thinking and operational discipline early. I learned to ask what fails, why it fails, and what recovery requires.
Those habits now carry into software work as attention to structure, reliability, and long-term maintainability.

sections.title: Why software is the direction now
paragraphs: The move toward software was not a hard pivot. It was a natural next step from understanding systems to building them more directly.
I am now focused on .NET, TypeScript, React, and API-driven development while keeping that infrastructure-aware lens.

sections.title: What kind of builder I want to become
paragraphs: I want to build software that is technically clear, operationally responsible, and honest about scope.
For me, a serious body of work means consistent quality, well-explained decisions, and steady progression over hype.

about.personal: I am from the Netherlands and currently studying HBO Informatica at Avans Hogeschool, after completing MBO Level 4 in IT. Day to day, I am focused on building work that is honest about where I am now and deliberate about where I am going.

projects.headline: Selected builds and technical explorations.
projects.opening: Most of these projects are currently in the architecture, research, or early implementation phase. I use them as vehicles to explore software engineering, product development, networking, security, and business-oriented problem solving.

categories.label: Featured project
categories.intro: The project that best represents my background, technical focus, and engineering depth.

items.title: OpenCaptive
items.what: OpenCaptive is an open-source captive portal platform that allows organizations to build customized network access experiences. It aims to provide businesses with flexible authentication, branding, and access management capabilities without relying on closed, vendor-locked systems.
items.why: My background in networking and systems administration sparked an interest in how users interact with network infrastructure. OpenCaptive explores the intersection between networking, software engineering, and business operations — a natural extension of my MBO background toward building productized software.

categories.label: Selected builds
categories.intro: Backend security tooling and a platform that translates systems thinking into real product problems.

items.title: Aegis.Auth
items.what: Aegis.Auth is a reusable authentication and authorization library for .NET applications. It aims to simplify identity management by providing common security functionality through a developer-friendly package.
items.why: Authentication is a requirement for nearly every modern application. Instead of rebuilding the same identity logic repeatedly, I wanted to explore creating a reusable security-focused foundation that could be integrated into multiple projects.
outcome: Secure token handling and session architecture
Building software for other developers to consume

items.title: StrengthSuite
items.what: StrengthSuite is a gym management platform designed to combine member administration, subscriptions, access control, and training-related functionality into a single system. The long-term vision is to connect software directly with physical gym infrastructure such as entrance gates, QR access, and membership validation.
items.why: The project started from an interest in solving real operational challenges faced by gyms while exploring how software can interact with physical environments. Rather than building another generic CRUD application, the goal was to design a platform that supports actual business workflows and secure facility access.
outcome: Designing software around real-world business processes
Connecting digital systems to physical infrastructure
Balancing product requirements with technical constraints

categories.label: Early concept
categories.intro: An idea not yet in active development, kept here to show the product thinking direction.

items.title: SynqWealth
items.what: A personal finance platform concept exploring how income, expenses, investments, savings, and net worth can be unified into a single honest financial view.
items.why: Financial software requires careful domain design before implementation. This project is currently focused on mapping out the data architecture and domain model rather than rushing to build features.
outcome: Designing data structures for long-term scalability
items.status: Concept and data-model exploration — not yet in active development

empty.title: Details being prepared
empty.body: This entry is intentionally held until the scope and implementation are ready to publish.

journey.headline: From infrastructure roots to software development.
journey.opening: This is not a resume. It is a progression where each stage built the next one.

entries.title: IT systems foundation
entries.narrative: My technical base started in support and infrastructure environments. I worked close to Linux systems, networking, troubleshooting, and practical operations. That context taught me to think in dependencies, failure paths, and operational consequences.
entries.forward: This stage built the habits that still shape how I approach software.

entries.title: Growing technical awareness
entries.narrative: Working with infrastructure made software questions more visible. I became increasingly interested in why applications were designed the way they were and how better structure could prevent recurring problems. That curiosity bridged operations and software direction.
entries.forward: The focus started shifting from fixing systems to building them.

entries.title: Formal software development education
entries.narrative: After completing MBO Level 4 in IT, I started HBO Informatica at Avans Hogeschool. Structured software education gave me stronger models for architecture, implementation, and communication. The infrastructure background changed how quickly those concepts became practical.
entries.forward: Software became the primary direction, built on an existing technical base.

entries.title: Projects and technical explorations
entries.narrative: Learning started turning into visible output through projects and exploration work. I began documenting decisions more clearly and evaluating trade-offs more intentionally. The goal shifted from finishing tasks to building coherent technical work.
entries.forward: This stage produced the first portfolio structure and technical references.

entries.title: Early-career, software-forward
entries.narrative: I am currently focused on .NET, TypeScript, React, and API-driven work while continuing to deepen systems awareness. The portfolio is still growing, and that growth is intentional. The next horizon is internship-level software engineering where foundation and direction can compound.
entries.forward: The trajectory is clear: foundation first, software forward.

contact.headline: Open to the right conversations.
contact.intro: I am open to internship enquiries, technical discussions, and collaborations that fit my current stage of development.
welcome: Conversations about .NET, TypeScript, React, APIs, and systems-aware engineering.
Collaborative work where learning, structure, and technical quality are central.
contact.response: I respond to relevant professional enquiries, usually within two working days.
